1: The covenant language of ch 2 Samuel 5:1 — 'we are your own bone and flesh' (ʿaṣmᵉḵā ûḇᵉśārᵉḵā ʾᵃnaḥnû) — is the kinship formula of covenant union (Genesis 2:23: Adam's 'bone of my bones and flesh of my flesh'). The tribes of Israel claim kinship with David as the basis for their submission. This language is taken up by Paul in Ephesians 5:30 for the believer's union with Christ: 'we are members of his body.' The people's covenantal bonding to the Davidic king is a type of the church's union with Christ — the kinship declared by the tribes is the same kinship that the NT declares to be constituted by the incarnation (Hebrews 2:14: 'since the children share in flesh and blood, he himself likewise partook of the same things').
2: The tribal leaders' appeal includes a direct prophetic citation: 'the LORD said to you, you shall shepherd my people Israel, and you shall be ruler over Israel.' The verb 'shepherd' (rāʿāh, to pasture/lead a flock) is the consistent OT metaphor for royal leadership (Psalms 78:70-72; Ezekiel 34:23). This specific oracle about David's shepherd-kingship is what Jesus claims in John 10:11-14 ('I am the good shepherd') and what Ezekiel 34:23-24 explicitly messianic-izes: 'I will set up over them one shepherd, my servant David, and he shall feed them.' The anointing of David as shepherd-king at Hebron is the OT narrative embodiment of what the NT presents as fulfilled in Christ's universal shepherding.
3: The covenant David makes with the elders of Israel at Hebron — 'wayyikrot dāwiḏ lāhem bᵉrît bᵉḥeḇrôn lipnê YHWH' — is the third and final anointing: the first was private (1 Samuel 16:13), the second tribal (2 Samuel 2:4), and this one is national. A covenant sealed before YHWH creates the formal, legally binding relationship between king and people. The typological chain points to the new covenant ratified by Christ's blood (Luke 22:20: 'this cup is the new covenant in my blood'), in which Christ as the Davidic heir establishes the binding relationship between himself and the new-covenant community.
4: The chronological note — 'David was thirty years old when he became king' — has been noticed by readers since the early church as an alignment with Luke 3:23: 'Jesus was about thirty years old when he began his ministry.' Luke may be deliberately invoking the Davidic parallel: the true heir of David begins his public work at the same age his type began to reign. The 'thirty years' marker thus functions as a shadow-pointer within Luke's chronological framing of Jesus's ministry as the fulfillment of Davidic kingship.
5: The summary of David's reigns — seven and a half years in Hebron, thirty-three in Jerusalem — totaling forty years establishes the forty-year reign as the OT's paradigmatic royal generation (cf. Solomon's forty years, 1 Kings 11:42). The number forty in OT idiom marks a complete period of trial and fulfillment (forty years wilderness, forty days of Moses on Sinai, forty years of peace under the judges). David's forty-year reign is the type of the 'forever' kingdom: forty years that the NT presents as the temporal shadow of the eternal reign (Revelation 11:15).
6: The Jebusites' taunt — 'you will not come in here; the blind and the lame will drive you away' — is enigmatic. Whatever its precise meaning, David captures the city. As a shadow: the stronghold held by those who mock the king's approach is taken by the king. The city that seemed impregnable is taken — the NT consistently uses this pattern for Christ's conquest: 'the gates of Hades will not prevail' (Matthew 16:18); the 'strong man's house' is ransacked (Matthew 12:29).
7: A type: 'David captured the stronghold of Zion, which is now the city of David.' Zion becomes the city of the Davidic king — and through the Psalter and the prophets, the seat of the eternal king and the place of eschatological gathering. Psalms 2:6 ('I have set my king on Zion, my holy hill') is the OT locus for this typology; Hebrews 12:22 ('you have come to Mount Zion') and Revelation 14:1 ('I looked, and behold, on Mount Zion stood the Lamb') make explicit that Zion as the seat of the Davidic king is fulfilled in Christ's heavenly kingship.
8: The enigmatic reference to the 'water tunnel' (ṣinnôr) and the 'blind and lame' is difficult historically but important typologically. Whatever the military tactic, the point is that the apparently inaccessible is accessed by the king who is determined to take the city. As a theme: Christ's approach to what human blindness declares inaccessible — the entry into human suffering and death — takes the stronghold by a route that the proud and powerful consider beneath contempt.
9: David settles in the stronghold and 'built up the city all around from the Millo inward.' The building program is the founding act of kingship: the king makes the city his own by inhabiting and improving it. As a type: Christ's 'building' program is the church — 'I will build my church' (Matthew 16:18) — and his inhabiting of Jerusalem is fulfilled in the heavenly Jerusalem he prepares as his dwelling (John 14:2-3; Revelation 21:2-3).
10: The theological summary: 'David grew steadily greater, for the LORD God of hosts was with him.' The phrase 'the LORD God of hosts was with him' (YHWH ʾĕlōhê ṣᵉḇāʾôt ʿimmô) is the OT's most concentrated version of the Immanuel promise — 'God with us.' The pattern of the king's greatness being entirely attributed to divine presence is the OT foundation for the NT's claim about Christ: 'God was with him' (Acts 10:38). As a theme: the Davidic king who grows in strength precisely because YHWH is with him prefigures the one in whom YHWH is not merely present with but is personally.
11: Hiram king of Tyre sends cedar timber and craftsmen to build David a palace. The Gentile king of the great Phoenician trading city places his resources at the Israelite king's disposal — a shadow of the eschatological vision in Isaiah 60:5-7, where the nations bring their wealth to Zion. The inclusion of Gentile resources in building the Davidic capital prefigures the NT vision of the nations being incorporated into the kingdom (Ephesians 2:11-22).
12: David's recognition — 'the LORD had established him as king over Israel and had elevated his kingdom for the sake of his people Israel' — is a statement of divine appointment for communal benefit. The king is established not for his own glory but 'for the sake of his people.' This pattern is the OT template for the NT's description of Christ's Davidic kingship: he does not seek the throne for himself but is exalted by the Father 'for us and for our salvation' (Nicene Creed; Philippians 2:9-11).
13: The brief notice that David took more wives and concubines in Jerusalem reflects the ANE practice of royal polygamy as political alliance-building. The narrative does not endorse this practice; it notes it as fact within David's reign. As a shadow pointing to the need for a better king: where David multiplies wives, the Deuteronomic law for kings explicitly prohibits it (Deuteronomy 17:17). The king 'after God's own heart' nonetheless fails to fully embody the royal ideal — a recurring shadow establishing the necessity of a sinless fulfillment.
14: The list of sons born in Jerusalem begins with Nathan — whose name will be associated with the Lukan genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:31 traces the line through Nathan, not Solomon). This alternative Davidic descent line through Nathan rather than Solomon is the genealogical foundation for the line from which Mary descends, ensuring that the incarnate Christ is biologically Davidic through Mary while legally royal-Davidic through Joseph. The son Nathan is a genealogical shadow in the middle of the list.
15: The continuation of the son list — Ibhar, Elishua, Nepheg, Japhia — is a record of the Davidic household at the height of its power in Jerusalem. As a theme: the multiplication of the Davidic line in Jerusalem is the OT's expression of covenant fruitfulness — 'your offspring shall be as the stars of heaven' — and finds its NT realization in the spiritual multiplication of those who become 'children of God' through Christ (John 1:12; Romans 8:17).
16: The completion of the Jerusalem son list: Elishama, Eliada, Eliphelet. This genealogical record of the Davidic household is not spiritually empty: genealogy in the OT is the structure through which God's covenant promises are tracked and transmitted. Every son listed is potentially in the line through which the promise of 2 Samuel 7:12 will be fulfilled. The list establishes the fertility and continuity of the Davidic house as the vehicle for covenant purposes.
17: When the Philistines hear that David has been anointed over all Israel, they come up in force 'to search for him.' The new king is immediately threatened by the dominant military power. The pattern: the anointed king's enthronement immediately provokes the attack of the enemy. This is the Psalms 2:1-3 pattern ('why do the nations rage... the kings of the earth set themselves against the LORD and against his anointed?'), fulfilled in the opposition Christ faces immediately after his messianic identity is established (Matthew 2:3-16; Revelation 12:1-6).
18: The Valley of Rephaim (ʿēmeq rᵉpāʾîm) — named for the Rephaim, the ancient 'giant' peoples of pre-Israelite Canaan — is the Philistine deployment zone. The choice of location by the Philistines invokes the memory of the pre-conquest inhabitants whom Israel was commanded to dispossess. The enemies of the Davidic king thus stand in the long lineage of those who oppose the divine claim on the land — a typological chain culminating in the NT's 'principalities and powers' who oppose Christ's kingdom (Ephesians 6:12).
19: David 'inquired of the LORD' before engaging the Philistines. This pattern of prophetic consultation (šāʾal bYHWH) before each battle is the defining characteristic of Davidic warfare — in contrast to Saul, whose failures were characterized by acting without prophetic authorization. As a theme: the king who acts only in dependence on YHWH's word is the OT pattern for Christ's 'I can do nothing on my own' (John 5:30) and 'I do nothing from myself, but speak just as the Father taught me' (John 8:28).
20: The victory at Baal-perazim — named 'Lord of the breach' after the breakthrough — elicits David's theological interpretation: 'The LORD has burst through my enemies before me like a bursting flood.' The verb pāraṣ (to break through, to burst) is used for YHWH's sudden, overwhelming intervention. As a revelation of God: YHWH as the one who 'breaks through' on behalf of his anointed establishes the pattern of divine irruption that the NT presents as fulfilled in Christ's resurrection — God breaking through the barrier of death on behalf of his Son (Acts 2:24).
21: The Philistines abandon their idols, which David and his men take away. The idols abandoned by the defeated enemy are captured by the victorious king — a reversal of the Ark's capture in 1 Sam 4-5. As a theme: the true king's victory results in the stripping of power from false gods (Colossians 2:15: 'he disarmed the rulers and authorities and put them to open shame, triumphing over them in him'). The Philistine idols left on the battlefield at Baal-perazim are a small-scale version of the disarming of 'every rule and authority and power' that 1 Corinthians 15:24 assigns to the Davidic Son at the end.
22: The Philistines return to the Valley of Rephaim — the same enemy, the same territory. The necessity of a second engagement reinforces that the king's victory over opposition is not a single event but a sustained campaign. As a theme: the warfare of the Davidic king is ongoing, not resolved by a single battle. The NT presents the same pattern for Christ's kingdom: 'he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet' (1 Corinthians 15:25), a present-tense ongoing process, not a completed act.
23: For the second battle, YHWH gives a different strategy: 'do not go straight up; circle around behind them and come at them in front of the balsam trees.' The command to attack from an unexpected direction — not the direct assault but the flanking movement — illustrates that the king who obeys YHWH receives specific guidance for each engagement rather than a general formula. As a theme: Christ's strategies in the NT are similarly non-conventional — feeding rather than fighting (John 6:35), forgiving rather than condemning (John 8:11), dying rather than escaping.
24: The signal for attack: 'when you hear the sound of marching in the tops of the balsam trees, rouse yourself to act, for then the LORD has gone out before you to strike the Philistine army.' YHWH himself is the advance guard; the king's action follows YHWH's initiative. As a revelation of God: the LORD 'goes out before' his anointed into battle — the divine warrior who leads the army. This prefigures Revelation 19:11-16, where Christ the warrior-king on the white horse goes out 'to judge and make war,' and the armies of heaven follow him.
25: David's obedience — 'he did as the LORD commanded him' — and the result: the Philistines struck from Geba to Gezer. The formula 'just as the LORD commanded' marks the completion of the obedience-victory cycle. As a type: the Davidic king who hears, obeys, and sees victory is the OT template for Christ's obedience: 'he became obedient to the point of death, even death on a cross. Therefore God has highly exalted him' (Philippians 2:8-9). The obedience of the son is the mechanism of the victory.
